concepts.title: What a limit actually is
concepts.explanation: A limit describes what value a function approaches as the input approaches some number — not what the value IS at that point. This distinction is crucial: a function can have a limit at a point where it is undefined. Limits are the foundation that makes calculus rigorous.

pitfalls.description: Assuming lim f(x) = f(a) always holds
pitfalls.whyCommon: For most everyday functions this is true (continuity), so students apply it universally and fail for piecewise or rational functions with holes.
pitfalls.fixStrategy: Always check if the function is continuous at the point first. If not, evaluate the limit from both sides separately.

realWorld.example: Instantaneous velocity is the limit of average velocity as the time interval shrinks to zero
Continuous compound interest uses the limit definition of e

concepts.title: The derivative as a rate of change
concepts.explanation: The derivative f'(x) tells you the instantaneous rate of change of f at any point x — it is the slope of the tangent line to the curve at that point. Every time you see 'rate of change' in the real world, a derivative is involved: speed (rate of change of position), acceleration (rate of change of speed), growth rate of a population.

concepts.title: The chain rule — derivatives of composed functions
concepts.explanation: When one function is nested inside another, the chain rule says: differentiate the outside function (leaving the inside unchanged), then multiply by the derivative of the inside. Visualise peeling layers of an onion — you handle each layer in turn.

pitfalls.description: Forgetting the chain rule for composite functions
pitfalls.whyCommon: Students correctly differentiate simple functions but apply those rules mechanically to composed functions, forgetting the inner derivative.
pitfalls.fixStrategy: Before differentiating, explicitly identify: what is the outer function? What is the inner function? Write them down before computing.

realWorld.example: Backpropagation in neural networks chains derivatives through every layer
Marginal cost and marginal revenue are derivatives of total cost and revenue functions
Drug concentration rate of change in bloodstream guides dosing intervals

concepts.title: Integration as area accumulation
concepts.explanation: The definite integral of f from a to b is the signed area between the curve and the x-axis. 'Signed' means area below the axis is negative. This is not just a geometric curiosity — accumulated area physically represents total distance traveled, total work done, total probability in a range, or total revenue over time.

pitfalls.description: Forgetting the constant of integration C for indefinite integrals
pitfalls.whyCommon: The mechanical process of applying integration rules works without C, so students omit it and lose marks.
pitfalls.fixStrategy: Write +C immediately after writing the integral sign, before doing any computation — make it a physical habit.

realWorld.example: Work done by a variable force = integral of force over displacement
P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = integral of the probability density function from a to b
